local_names.romanized_local: Bendi masak (Malay); Vendakkai samaithadu (Tamil); Bhindi paki hui (Hindi)

region_of_origin: Ethiopia and Northeast Africa (origin); widely cultivated across South Asia, Southeast Asia, and Singapore
food_subgroup: Cooked okra pods (Abelmoschus esculentus) — very low calorie, high mucilaginous dietary fibre; boiled or stir-fried preparation

ontology.foodex2_description: Okra pods (Abelmoschus esculentus), cooked, boiled without salt
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00OK represents okra pods cooked/boiled without salt; used as closest parent as no separate stir-fried variant code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cooked okra pod food product; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.description: Per 100g cooked / 8–10 pods (typical Singapore hawker side serving)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical Singapore hawker side serving of cooked lady's fingers is approximately 8–10 pods (about 100g), served stir-fried with sambal, garlic, or plain boiled as a vegetable side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Okra Cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11278
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy Foods Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FDC SR Legacy #11278 reports cooked okra (boiled, drained, without salt) per 100g. Values used directly. This is an approved primary source for raw ingredients and staples.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Okra — peer-reviewed estimate based on mucilaginous fibre content; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~20 for cooked okra is a well-supported estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Okra's high mucilaginous (viscous soluble) fibre content significantly slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption, yielding a very low GI. GL per 100g serving = GI × available carbohydrate / 100 = 20 × 5.0 / 100 = 1.0; GL per serving rounded to 0.4 reflects typical 50g portion equivalent. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (7mg/100g), high potassium (299mg/100g), and high calcium (77mg/100g) support blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Plain cooked okra is highly suitable for hypertensive patients: sodium is negligible at 7mg/100g, potassium at 299mg/100g supports vasodilation, and calcium at 77mg/100g (one of the highest among vegetables) contributes to vascular tone; avoid high-sodium preparations such as sambal or salted stir-fry sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~20) and negligible glycemic load; mucilaginous fibre slows glucose absorption; METFORMIN INTERACTION WARNING for raw okra/okra water
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cooked okra is highly suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low GI (~20) and negligible glycemic load; however, patients on metformin must be advised that raw okra and okra water significantly reduce metformin bioavailability — cooked okra in normal serving sizes is generally safe, but okra water or raw okra consumption alongside metformin should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0g), zero cholesterol, high soluble fibre (mucilage) with bile acid-binding properties supporting LDL reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cooked okra is cardioprotective: it contains zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and its mucilaginous soluble fibre binds bile acids in the gut, promoting LDL cholesterol excretion; potassium (299mg/100g) and folate (37mcg DFE/100g) further support cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Calcium (77mg/100g) relevant for corticosteroid-induced osteoporosis risk; folate (37mcg DFE) important for methotrexate-treated RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cooked okra is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients: calcium at 77mg/100g (approximately 8% RDI) is clinically relevant for patients on long-term corticosteroids at risk of osteoporosis, vitamin C (23mg/100g) supports collagen synthesis, and folate (37mcg DFE/100g) is important for patients on methotrexate therapy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lady's fingers (okra), cooked — per 100g (boiled or stir-fried, drained, without salt): approximately 22 kcal (very low calorie), 1.9g protein, 0.2g fat (negligible), 5g carbohydrate, 3.2g dietary fibre (predominantly mucilaginous soluble fibre). Sodium is negligible at 7mg/100g. GI ~20 (very low), glycemic load negligible. Okra (Abelmoschus esculentus) is a green ridged pod vegetable with a mild earthy taste and characteristic mucilaginous (slimy) texture when cooked, which is the result of its high soluble fibre content. In Singapore, it is commonly sold at Indian and Malay hawker stalls as sambal bendi (stir-fried with sambal chilli), stir-fried with garlic, or served in vegetable curries. Known as bendi in Malay, vendakkai in Tamil, bhindi in Hindi, and 秋葵 / 羊角豆 in Chinese. Nutritionally notable for its calcium content (~77mg/100g — among the highest of any vegetable), potassium (299mg/100g), folate (37mcg DFE/100g), and vitamin C (23mg/100g). CLINICAL ALERT — METFORMIN INTERACTION: Raw okra and okra water significantly reduce metformin bioavailability; patients on metformin should avoid consuming raw okra water alongside their medication. Cooked okra in normal serving sizes is generally safe. Suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets. Source: USDA FDC SR Legacy #11278. Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: 羊角豆 秋葵 glycaemic index

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11278 (cooked okra, boiled, drained, without salt), an approved primary source. GI estimate of ~20 is well-supported by University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from the approved source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture content for cooked okra not separately reported in USDA FDC SR Legacy #11278 as of 2026-04-14. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FDC #11278). saturated_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FDC #11278). cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food). vitamin_b12_mcg reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food; confirmed per USDA FDC #11278). vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0 (naturally zero for this vegetable; confirmed per USDA FDC #11278). METFORMIN INTERACTION NOTE: Raw okra and okra water significantly reduce metformin bioavailability; this record covers cooked okra (boiled/stir-fried) which is generally safe in normal serving sizes. GI source is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); confidence 0.9. FoodEx2 code A00OK used as closest parent (no separate stir-fried okra code exists). SNOMED CT code left null — no specific code identified for cooked okra food product.
